address: Na Phra Lan Rd, Phra Borom Maha Ratchawang, Phra Nakhon

description.en:
## Overview

The Grand Palace is Bangkok's most famous landmark and Thailand's most visited attraction. This sprawling 218,000-square-meter complex on the banks of the Chao Phraya River served as the official residence of the Kings of Siam from 1782 to 1925. Within its walls lies Wat Phra Kaew (Temple of the Emerald Buddha), home to Thailand's most sacred religious artifact — the Phra Kaew Morakot, a 66-centimeter Buddha image carved from a single piece of green jade. The intricate gold-leaf architecture, glittering mosaic decorations, and mythological guardian statues make this one of the most visually overwhelming sites in Southeast Asia.

## History

King Rama I established the Grand Palace in 1782 when he moved the capital from Thonburi across the river to Bangkok. Every subsequent king added buildings and halls, creating the eclectic mix of Thai, European, and Khmer architectural styles visible today. The Chakri Maha Prasat Throne Hall, built by King Chulalongkorn (Rama V) in 1882, famously blends an Italian Renaissance body with a traditional Thai multi-tiered roof — leading locals to joke it looks like a foreigner wearing a Thai hat. While the royal family moved to Chitralada Palace in the 1920s, the Grand Palace remains the ceremonial heart of the Thai monarchy and is used for coronations, state receptions, and royal funerals.

## What to See

The **Emerald Buddha** (Phra Kaew Morakot) inside Wat Phra Kaew is the spiritual highlight — the king personally changes its golden seasonal costume three times a year to mark summer, rainy season, and winter. The **murals of the Ramakien** line the gallery walls surrounding the temple, depicting Thailand's version of the Hindu epic Ramayana across 178 panels. The **Chakri Maha Prasat Throne Hall** impresses with its European-Thai hybrid architecture. The **Dusit Maha Prasat Hall**, the oldest building in the complex, showcases pure traditional Thai architecture with its distinctive four-tiered roof.

## Practical Info

- **Entry fee:** 500 THB (includes Wat Phra Kaew); children under 120 cm enter free
- **Opening hours:** 08:30-15:30 daily (closed during royal ceremonies)
- **How to get there:** Take the Chao Phraya Express Boat to Tha Chang pier (N9), or MRT to Sanam Chai station (5-minute walk)
- **Visit duration:** 2-3 hours
- **Dress code:** Strictly enforced — long pants, covered shoulders, no sandals; free loaner clothing available

best_time_to_visit: Arrive at 8:30 AM opening to beat the tour bus crowds; mornings are also cooler
tips: Ignore anyone outside the gate who tells you the palace is closed today — this is a well-known scam to redirect you to gem shops
Photography is allowed outdoors but prohibited inside all buildings including the Emerald Buddha temple
Wear long pants and covered shoes to avoid delays at the strict dress code checkpoint
